nodes.body: The rowboat scraped onto the sand at first light, and Wren climbed out with a satchel, a flask, and one blank sheet of paper. The mate pointed at the island and made the rule plain. Fill that paper with a true chart before the ship returns at dusk, or the apprenticeship goes to somebody else. Then the oars dipped and the little boat pulled away, and Wren stood alone on a shore that no map had ever named. Five separate ways led inland from the beach. A tangled mangrove coast steamed away to the left, and a green forest ridge climbed directly ahead. Dark caverns breathed cool air from below, while a leaning survey tower waited out on the headland. And to the right, wide tidal flats glittered like a mirror. Wren breathed slowly and weighed the choices.

choices.label: Wade into the mangrove coast.
Climb toward the cloud-forest ridge.
Descend into the sink-hole caverns.
Cross to the ruined survey tower.
Walk out onto the tidal flats.

nodes.body: The mangrove coast was a maze of roots and slow brown water. Herons stood among the branches, still as sticks, waiting for fish. Wren waded in carefully, testing the mud before each stride, because one wrong step could swallow a boot to the knee. The morning would not last, and neither would the low water. To draw anything true, Wren needed a bearing, and three of them offered themselves. Upcoast, a fallen log crossed a channel like a green bridge. Downcoast, a cluster of oyster rocks broke the shallows. Straight inland, a hidden creek slid away under the leaves. And nearby, a still green lagoon glimmered, asking to be crossed.

choices.label: Follow the log bridge upcoast.
Pick along the oyster rocks downcoast.
Push inland up the hidden creek.
Wade toward the still green lagoon.

nodes.body: The fallen log was slippery and furred with green, and it crossed a channel where the tide had already begun to turn. Wren went slowly, arms out for balance, boots gripping the wet bark. Halfway across, something caught the eye. A knot of driftwood was wedged against the log, and its pale surface had been carved with old marks, faint but deliberate. On the far bank a heron rookery clattered and squawked among the branches. Wren paused, one foot forward, the tide sliding below. The carving might mean something, or it might mean nothing at all. But the water was rising, and a greasy log was no place to linger.

choices.label: Stop to copy the carved driftwood.
Hurry across before the tide.

nodes.body: Wren crouched low and looked at the driftwood. The marks were a mapmaker's shorthand, arrows and a small compass rose. Someone had scratched them here a very long time ago, and the wood had greyed around them. Two of the arrows pointed clearly. One sent a traveller back toward the sea caves along the shore. The other pointed up, toward higher ground away from the swamp. It was a message left on purpose, Wren felt sure, a hand reaching across the years. There was time to trust the arrow and go, or time to read the whole message slowly and be certain of it.

choices.label: Trust the arrow toward high ground.
Read the marks slowly for a message.

nodes.body: Wren read the marks slowly, the way the Chart House had taught, and the shorthand settled into sense. It really was a message. The final strokes spelled a name, Alder, cut small into the grey wood, and below the name ran a promise. A complete chart of the island had been left at a camp on the ridge, waiting there for anyone who came after. The arrow up the hill was clear and certain, drawn twice for emphasis. Whoever Alder had been, they had wanted to be followed. Wren traced the name once with a fingertip, then looked toward the rising ground and the path Alder had marked.

choices.label: Head for high ground as Alder marked.

nodes.body: Wren stepped off the log just as the rookery burst up. Hundreds of herons rose together in a storm of grey wings and cries. In the racket it was hard to think and harder to see, and Wren nearly missed the path splitting ahead. One branch went left across a shining mud slick, quick but risky. The other kept right, climbing through firm palms on solid ground. The birds wheeled and settled, then wheeled again. Wren wiped a splash of mud from one cheek. The two ways deserved a careful look, because the wrong choice here could cost the whole morning.

choices.label: Cross the mud slick quickly.
Keep to the firm palms.

nodes.body: The mud slick was far deeper than it looked, and one stride was enough to grip Wren's boot like thick glue. Then the other leg sank past the knee. Wren twisted and hauled and wriggled, breathing hard, until at last a boot pulled free with a sucking pop, filthy to the thigh. But the struggle had already cost precious minutes. Behind, the tide had quietly closed over the log bridge, and this whole finger of coast was cut off now, a corner Wren could not reach. It would stay a blank patch on the paper. Wren backed toward firmer ground and started the long way round, wiser and much muddier than before.

The ground lifted at last out of the swamp, and mud gave way to firm palms and dry leaves underfoot. Wren climbed a low rise and paused to breathe. From here the whole northern shape of the island opened up. A long curl of reef lay offshore, and tucked into the cliffs shone a bright notch of calm water that could only be a hidden cove. It was the first real view of the day, and it made the map feel possible at last. A goat track wound onward ahead, one arm dropping toward the shining cove. The other angled inland toward a green cleft where a spring gully seemed to run.

choices.label: Take the goat track down to the cove.
Angle inland toward a spring gully.

nodes.body: The oyster rocks were sharp underfoot and busy with crabs. They scuttled sideways into every crack. Wren picked along carefully, minding both hands and boots on the cutting shells. Among the rocks lay a shape that did not belong. A small sea turtle had been tipped onto its back in a shrinking pool, its flippers waving at the sky. The falling water had left it stranded. It was not hurt, only stuck, and the pools were draining toward the sea. Wren could stop to study the creature and read the tide, or search on among the rocks for a way inland.

choices.label: Study the turtle and read the tide pools.
Search the rocks for a way inland.

nodes.body: Up close, the turtle was perfectly fine, only stuck, its dark eye rolling to watch Wren. Righting it now would be the kind thing to do, but the tide was dropping fast, and every minute here was a minute stolen from the map. There was another way to help. Wren could mark the turtle's exact spot and sketch the reef channel that fed the pools, then send help back once a high place was found and the ship could be signalled. A quick rescue, or a careful note and a promise kept later. Wren looked from the waving flippers to the wide unmapped island and weighed the two.

choices.label: Mark the turtle and press on inland.
Stop and right the turtle now.

nodes.body: Wren knelt and tipped the turtle upright, and it found its feet at once and scuttled into the sea, gone in a swirl of green. It was a good thing to have done, and Wren grinned to watch it disappear. But the delay let the tide flood back over the rocks, and with soaked paper and slipping light there was time to map only this corner. Wren drew the reef honestly, every channel and rock recorded true, a small square of coast finished well. The rest of the island stayed blank behind it. It was not the chart the mate wanted, perhaps, but it was an honest sketch and a real kindness.

Past the oyster rocks, a thread of clear water crossed Wren's path, threading inland through the salt marsh. Wren cupped a handful and tasted it, cold and sweet, and colder still the higher it climbed. That meant a spring somewhere above the swamp was feeding it. Fresh water always came down from higher ground, and higher ground was exactly where a mapmaker needed to be. The seep made a natural guide, a silver line to follow up out of the mud. Wren shouldered the satchel and set off along it, glad of a clear direction at last.

choices.label: Follow the clear seep upward.

nodes.body: The seep of clear water climbed out of the marsh into a shaded gully of ferns, and the air turned cool and green. The water was properly cold now, tumbling over dark stones, so Wren stopped to fill the flask before going on. Ahead the gully divided. One arm climbed toward a bright gleam that could only be the spring pool itself, while the other bent aside beneath a mossy overhang hung thick with vines. It was a shadowed place, and it looked as though it might shelter more than ferns. Both ways led upward, away from the swamp. Wren listened to the trickling water and chose a fork.

choices.label: Climb to the bright spring pool.
Duck under the mossy overhang.

nodes.body: Wren ducked in beneath the mossy overhang, where the rock was dry, beyond the reach of rain and tide. Somebody had sheltered in this spot before. A ring of blackened stones marked an old firepit, and beside it lay a dented tin cup, long emptied. On the stone wall, scratched deep, was a single word, Alder, and below it an arrow pointing uphill toward what the marks named a camp. Wren ran a thumb across the letters. Whoever Alder was, they had passed this way and wanted to be followed. The shelter was snug and the day was long, but that arrow tugged at Wren, uphill, toward the camp.

choices.label: Follow Alder's arrow toward the camp.
Copy the firepit shelter and rest a while.

nodes.body: The dry shelter was welcome after the swamp, and Wren settled down to sketch it. The firepit, the cup, the scratched name. The pencil moved happily along, and the morning slipped by unnoticed under the mossy roof. When the light finally shifted across the wall, Wren looked up with a start. Only this one snug corner was drawn, careful and small. The whole island lay bare beyond it. Wren scrambled up and brushed away the dust, then hurried out into the gully. The sun was already too high, and far too much paper was still white.

The hidden creek ran inland straight and slow, beneath a green roof of leaves, so quiet that Wren could hear the water lapping the roots. It made easy, level going after the swamp. Around a bend came a soft splashing, and there they were, a mother otter and two round kits, watching from a half-sunk log. For a moment nobody moved. Then the otters slipped off the log and vanished upstream in a line of silver bubbles. Wren smiled and considered the ways onward. The otters had gone up the creek, firm trees rose off to one side, and across the water a loud heron island crowded the channel with wings.

choices.label: Follow the otters upstream.
Cut away from the water into the trees.
Pole across to the loud heron island.

nodes.body: Upstream the creek shallowed over a clean gravel bar. Clear water ran bright across the stones, and otters played in the riffles below. Beyond the bar the ground rose firm and dry, above the reach of the tide at last. Wren cupped a drink from the creek. It tasted sweet, not salt, fed from somewhere higher up the island. Fresh water climbing meant a spring above, and a spring meant high ground, and high ground meant a view. Wren stepped up onto the dry bank and followed the sweet water on, glad to leave the mud behind.

choices.label: Climb along the sweet water.

nodes.body: Away from the creek the trees closed in, and the light went green and dim beneath them. Without the water to follow, Wren had to choose a heading by instinct alone, which was just the sort of thing that lost people in a mangrove. Off to one side and uphill, a faint brightness showed between the trunks, promising open ground and a view. The other way, a level game trail ran off flat and easy, tempting because it asked so little. Wren stood still and listened for a while. Easy was not always right, and bright was not always near. One way would lead out, and the other might only lead deeper.

choices.label: Climb toward the brightness.
Take the level game trail.

nodes.body: The game trail ran level and easy, exactly as it had promised, and for a while Wren made good progress. But it kept curling back on itself, bending left, then left again, until with a cold jolt Wren understood the trouble. There was no true landmark here, no rise, no shore, only more mangrove in every direction, all of it identical. The trail could be a loop, and a loop went nowhere. Behind and above, that patch of brightness still showed through the leaves, the one thing that was not more of the same. Wren could turn back toward it now, or gamble on one more bend.

choices.label: Backtrack toward the bright rise.
Gamble on one more bend.

nodes.body: Wren took the gamble and pushed on around a final bend, and the trail gave onto a fallen tree, half rotted and unmistakable. It was the very same tree Wren had passed an hour before. The mangrove had been walking Wren in a slow, patient circle the whole time. The sun stood high now, and there was no undoing the wasted hour. Wren drew the coast as well as it could be guessed, an honest rough shape with the middle left blank, because a map you cannot really see is no map at all. Then Wren turned back toward firmer ground, ready to begin again where the land could be seen.

The cloud-forest ridge climbed straight up off the beach into cool grey mist. Moss dripped from every branch, and birdsong fell from somewhere unseen overhead. It was a green, dripping world, and it smelled of wet earth. Wren started up, and soon the ways parted. A well-worn switchback climbed steeply, zigzagging up the face of the slope. A gentler traverse angled across the hillside, longer but kinder. A side path dropped to a mossy hollow where something pale gleamed. And through the trees came a low roar, the sound of falling water. Wren wiped mist from both eyes and chose a way up.

choices.label: Take the steep switchback up.
Angle across the gentle traverse.
Drop to the pale gleam in the hollow.
Follow the roar toward a hidden waterfall.

nodes.body: The switchback was steep but honest, with no tricks in it. Each bend opened a wider view of the island falling away below. Wren stopped to breathe on a flat ledge where the path went two ways. Straight up rose a bare rock rib, exposed and airy, a quick hard line for anyone bold enough. Curving off to the side ran a green shelf, level and sheltered, tracing toward a break in the trees. Both climbed toward the ridge above. Wren caught a breath, checked the footing, and picked the way that suited the legs and the nerve.

choices.label: Climb the bare rock rib.
Follow the green shelf.

nodes.body: The rock rib was airy and exposed, with cloud streaming past on either side. Wren went up carefully, testing each hold before trusting it with weight. It was frightening in the best way, all height and open air, and it gained ground fast. Hand over hand, boot after boot, Wren climbed. The rib delivered onto a broad grassy bench where the true ridge began. Here the wind combed the tussocks, and the world spread out below. A worn path ran on toward higher ground, the sure sign of feet that had passed this way many times. Wren stood tall, heart still pounding, and followed it.

choices.label: Follow the ridge path higher.

nodes.body: The green shelf traced a level line across the slope. The more Wren walked it, the plainer its purpose became. It held its height exactly, curving patiently around every fold of the hill. This was a contour path, the very sort a surveyor cuts to move sideways without losing hard-won height. Someone had made it on purpose, long ago, and used it often. Wren followed it around the shoulder of the slope, glad of the easy footing. It brought them out, sure enough, onto the same grassy bench where the ridge path began its long climb toward the high ground.

choices.label: Join the ridge path.

nodes.body: The grassy bench was the ridge's broad shoulder, and up here the wind was a live thing. It combed the tussocks flat and tugged at Wren's satchel. The mist had thinned, and the whole spine of the island lifted ahead. A stony trail climbed on toward the high camp Wren kept hearing about, the place every clue pointed to. Off to one side, though, stood something else. A hush of giant mossy trees marked an ancient grove, older and stiller than the rest of the forest. The trail called upward, plain and purposeful. The grove called sideways, quiet and strange.

choices.label: Climb the stony trail toward the camp.
Detour into the grove of ancient trees.

nodes.body: The last stretch toward the camp was a scramble over broken rock, where the path all but vanished. It would have been easy to lose the way here and waste an hour. But someone careful had come before. A line of small cairns marched up the slope ahead, each little pile of stones set by a steady hand. They pointed the safe way through the rubble. Wren followed them from one to the next, trusting the pattern, hands finding holds where the cairns said to look. They led surely upward, out of the broken ground, on toward the high ridge where the camp had to stand.

choices.label: Follow the cairns onto the high ridge.

nodes.body: Up on the open ridge the wind was a wall, and the world fell away on both sides. It was thrilling and a little terrifying at once. The cairns marched on ahead, small grey markers stepping off toward a far speck that had to be the camp. The ground rose in long tired steps of rock and heather, up and up. Each rise revealed another beyond it. Wren tightened the satchel strap and leaned into the wind. Then Wren kept to the line of cairns, one careful stone at a time, toward that distant grey shape.

choices.label: Push on along the cairned ridge.

nodes.body: The ridge narrowed and split around a shoulder of bare rock, and Wren stopped to read the ground. The cairns went left, tracing a sheltered ledge that curled below the crest, safely back from the drop. A bolder line went right, straight over the airy crest itself, where the whole island lay spread on both sides. One way was patient and sure. The other was quicker and grander and far more exposed. And a third feeling rose too, the plain urge to stop here, daunted by the height, and go no further at all.

choices.label: Take the sheltered ledge.
Brave the airy crest.
Stop here, daunted by the height.

nodes.body: The drop fell away on both sides, sheer and dizzying, and Wren's head began to swim. The wind seemed to lean in and pull. For a long frozen minute Wren could not make either foot move forward. The only honest thing left was to sit down, breathe, and edge slowly back the safe way. That was not cowardice, whatever it felt like. A mapmaker who freezes on a high ridge and pushes on anyway is a mapmaker who falls. Wren mapped the ridge carefully from lower down, and left the high camp a grey speck out of reach, its chart saved for a braver day.

Wren took the sheltered ledge, and it was slow but sure, tucked below the crest out of the worst of the wind. The going was patient work, one steady step after another, with a hand always resting on the rock. Cushions of tiny alpine flowers grew in the cracks, pink and white, clinging where almost nothing else could. Wren passed them carefully, minding each foothold. The ledge held true. Little by little it climbed, threading below the ridgeline, until at last it lifted back to the crest where the two ways came together again. Wren stepped up, glad of the safe road taken.

choices.label: Rejoin the ridgeline.

nodes.body: Wren braved the airy crest, and it was pure exhilaration from the first step. This was the very spine of the island. On both hands the world dropped away, and cloud poured silently over from the far side like a slow white river. Wren went carefully, arms out, eyes on the footing, but grinning all the while. There was nowhere higher or wilder to be. The wind pushed and the cloud streamed and the crest ran on. Then it eased and broadened, and Wren came down breathless and beaming to a level shelf where the cairns gathered again on the flat ground.

choices.label: Come down to the level shelf.

nodes.body: Here the two ways came back together on a broad level shelf. The last cairns clustered thick, as if many travellers had paused in this same spot. The ground was flat and blessedly wide after the narrow climbing, and the wind dropped for a moment in the lee of a low rise. Wren stood among the little stone piles and looked up. One short pull remained, over grass and rock, toward the cliff where the camp had to stand. It was close now, close enough to feel. Wren shouldered the satchel, checked the sketchbook was dry, and made ready for the final climb.

choices.label: Make the final pull to the camp.

nodes.body: And there, at last, it was. Tucked against the base of the cliff, sheltered from the worst of the wind, stood a low camp of grey canvas and stone. Alder's camp, exactly where every clue on the island had been quietly pointing all day. The driftwood arrows, the scratched names, the line of patient cairns, all of them had led here. Wren stopped and simply looked at it for a moment, hardly believing the climb was done. Then, heart lifting, Wren crossed the last stretch of grass and rock toward the low doorway and stepped up to the threshold.

choices.label: Step into Alder's camp.

nodes.body: The traverse was gentle but long, angling across the wooded slope. It crossed one small stream after another, every one running the same way, downhill toward the sea. Wren read that pattern the way a mapmaker learns to. Follow water up, and it leads to its source. So Wren traced one stream upward against its flow, and it opened into a clear cold pool ringed with ferns. This, plainly, was the island's spring. Wren could rest here at the heart of it, keep on across the slope, or follow a narrow deer path aside toward a hidden dell.

choices.label: Rest at the spring.
Keep traversing along the slope.
Follow a deer path to a hidden dell.

nodes.body: Higher up the slope the mist thinned to rags, and a stand of wind-bent trees gave way to open ground. The light came clean and grey. Wren paused to get a bearing. A faint path climbed on from here, up toward the ridge and the high country where the real views waited. And below it, off to one side, a mossy hollow showed the same pale gleam that had winked up from the beach that morning. Both pulled at Wren. Up meant height and progress and the camp beyond. Down meant the pale mystery, whatever it was, waiting in the shadow of the hollow.

choices.label: Climb the faint path on.
Drop to the pale gleam.

nodes.body: Wren dropped down toward the pale gleam, and it turned out to be a curtain of white orchids over a rock face. Hundreds of small blooms glowed in the green shade, beautiful enough to stop the breath. But behind them, when Wren parted the flowers gently, there was more. A dry cleft ran back into the rock, and wedged into it sat a tin box. It was green with age and furred with moss, its lid marked with a small compass rose. Someone had hidden it here on purpose. Open it, or leave it and climb back up to the higher ground.

choices.label: Open the tin box.
Leave the box and climb back up.

nodes.body: The tin box came free with a soft grating sound, and Wren eased the lid up. Inside, kept dry all these years, lay folded papers, a stub of pencil, and a small brass locket gone dark with age. Wren unfolded the top paper, and it was field notes, written in a careful, practiced hand. The locket, when it clicked open, held a faded picture of a girl. She was young, half-smiling, with something in the eyes that looked a little like Wren's own. Two mysteries in one box. Read the notes through, or pocket the locket and hurry on.

choices.label: Read the field notes.
Pocket the locket and hurry on.

nodes.body: Wren slipped the little brass locket into a pocket and climbed on, but the girl in the faded picture would not leave. Who was she? Why left here, in a tin, behind a curtain of orchids? Wren turned it over and over the whole morning, eyes on the ground, mind far away. The ridge got mapped in a distracted, wandering scrawl that would shame a first-year student. It was not the day's work the mate had asked for. But some questions weigh more than a finished chart. Wren carried the small mystery home instead, the locket warm in one pocket and the map barely begun.

Wren read the field notes through, and the careful hand had a name at the bottom, Alder, the mapmaker who had come before. The notes told a plain, sad, hopeful story. Alder had drawn a full chart of the island and hidden it safe at a camp on the ridge. There was a warning too, underlined twice. The caverns below flooded fast and deep at high tide, and no one should be caught in them. The last line pointed simply uphill, toward the camp and the finished map. Wren folded the notes away, took heart from them, and turned to follow Alder's trail up.

choices.label: Follow Alder's notes toward the camp.

nodes.body: A cool breath of air rose out of the sink-hole, ringed with dripping ferns. Wren felt it on both cheeks like the island exhaling. Someone had been here before, and recently. A knotted rope hung down into the dark, tied off to a sturdy root at the rim. Wren tested the anchor with a hard pull, checked the knots, and climbed down hand over hand. The shaft opened into a cavern where three passages led away. From one came the steady echo of dripping water. Another glittered faintly, and the third breathed warm air from deeper in. High in the roof, a hole of pale daylight promised a way back up.

choices.label: Follow the dripping water.
Head toward the glitter.
Trace the warm breathing air.
Climb toward a high hole of daylight.

nodes.body: The dripping passage sloped down to an underground stream, clear and cold, running fast toward a low arch. Wren crouched to drink and read the walls the way a careful explorer must. High on one side ran a scratched line, deliberate and plain. It marked exactly where the water rose at the turn of the tide, a warning left by whoever came before. Wren noted it well and stayed below it. From here the stream offered three ways. Wade down with the current toward the low arch. Climb up against the flow toward wherever the water was born. Or slip aside into a narrow rift lit with glowworms.

choices.label: Wade the stream toward the arch.
Climb the stream upward against the flow.
Explore a side rift lit with glowworms.

nodes.body: The arch was low, and the water beneath it was rising, sliding up the rock in slow cold pulses. Wren stopped and thought hard, because a rising tide in a cave is nothing to gamble with. Beside the arch a dry ledge ran on above the water, curving toward a paler light ahead. That way stayed clear of the flood. But under the arch itself the passage bored straight into the dark heart of the caves, a shortcut, if it could be crossed in time. Wren watched the water climb another inch. The high dry ledge, or the low dark arch, and only one respected the tide.

choices.label: Edge along the dry ledge to the light.
Duck under the low arch.

nodes.body: Wren ducked under the low arch, and past it the roof dropped until it hung a hand's width above the water. For a heartbeat Wren hesitated, judging the gap. In that heartbeat the tide pushed in. The passage began to fill, cold water climbing the walls fast, exactly as the scratched line had warned. There was just time to scramble back, gasping, soaked to the chest. Wren hauled out under the arch as the water rose behind. Then Wren stood shaking on the dry ledge, heart slamming, and let the fright teach its lesson. The caverns became a single dark question mark on the map.

The way to the spring ran upstream, against the cold current, and Wren climbed it steadily, boots braced on the streambed. The higher the climb, the sweeter the water tasted. The roof lifted overhead. At last the passage opened into a bright chamber where a shaft of daylight fell straight down from a crack far above. It lit a still pool at the bottom. Wren stood in that shaft of light and understood. This was the underground face of the island's spring, where the fresh water first welled up out of the rock. The daylight climbed away above, toward the world of green and wind.

choices.label: Climb the daylight shaft to the spring.

nodes.body: The glitter was not treasure, and Wren felt a small tug of disappointment before the wonder took over. It was crystal, veins of clear quartz webbing the cavern walls, throwing the faint light back in a thousand sparks. But among the crystal was something far older. Handprints, pressed in red ochre onto the smooth stone, faded but plain. Whole and open, a scatter of them across the wall. Someone had marked this place long before any mapmaker came, before charts existed at all. Wren stared, moved. Copy the handprints carefully, or push on deeper toward a further glow in the dark.

choices.label: Copy the handprints carefully.
Push past toward a further glow.

nodes.body: Wren began copying the handprints, and as the pencil traced them a pattern came clear. The prints were not scattered at random. Together they made a rough shape, and the shape was the island itself, drawn by pressing living hands against the stone. There was a mark where the spring rose, and another at the high peak, the two hearts of the place. It was a map, the oldest map of all, made by the first people to find this island. It was older than the Chart House, older than Alder, older than everyone. Follow the ochre mark toward the spring, or fill a whole page with the ancient painting.

choices.label: Follow the ochre mark toward the spring.
Fill a whole page with the old painting.

nodes.body: Wren filled a whole page with the ochre handprints and the island painted around them. Every print, every faded line, copied true. It was a record no chart had ever held, proof in red earth that people had mapped here long before any surveyor. The Chart House would never have seen its like. It cost the afternoon, and the land above stayed half drawn. But when the keepers gathered around Wren's page and understood what it showed, the hush in the room said the trade had been fair. Some maps are older than maps, and worth the whole day.

Wren pushed toward the glow, and it grew from a thread to a crack to a bright seam of daylight. Squeezing up toward it, boots scrabbling, Wren tumbled out blinking into open air. A hidden shelf of beach lay ringed all around by high cliffs. It was the hidden cove, seen from below now, its calm water lapping quiet inside the reef. Wren stood in the sudden brightness, half laughing with relief. Back the way the passage had come, a side branch murmured with lapping water, deeper and darker. Ahead lay the cove itself, and a way up. Wren looked from the light to the dark and chose.

choices.label: Climb up into the cove.
Follow the passage toward the lapping water.

nodes.body: The warm passage breathed in and out like a sleeping animal, and around a bend Wren found the reason. A colony of little bats hung from the roof, packed close and softly stirring. The warmth was the warmth of all their small bodies together. But there was something else on a low ledge below them. A young seal pup lay curled there, far from any sea, its dark eyes catching the dim light. It had wriggled in through some flooded entrance and now could not climb back out. Wren crouched slowly, not wanting to frighten it. Study how the pup had come in, or back quietly away.

choices.label: Study how the pup got in.
Back away and leave the warm dark.

nodes.body: Wren studied the pup and the ledge and worked out the story. It had come through a flooded tube from the sea, then been stranded when the low tide drained away. It was frightened but unhurt. Wren could not lift it alone, that much was plain, because a seal pup is heavier and stronger than it looks. But there was another way to help. Mark the exact spot on the map, climb to a high place, and signal the ship, and grown hands might come before dusk to carry it out. A careful plan and a promise kept, or a lone and likely hopeless try at coaxing the pup free right now.

choices.label: Mark the pup and climb toward the light.
Try to coax the pup out alone now.

nodes.body: Wren tried to coax the pup out alone, and it was hopeless from the start. The pup was heavier than it looked and frightened besides, twisting and barking. The wet rock gave no grip. After a long slippery struggle that got them both nowhere, Wren had to sit back, breathing hard, and admit the truth. This needed more than one pair of hands. Wren marked the sea tube carefully on the map, so help could find it, and backed away. The rescue would have to wait for the rising tide and better luck. It was hard to walk away, but the wrong kind of trying only makes things worse.

Wren backed slowly out of the warm dark, careful not to startle the sleeping bats. Feeling along the passage, Wren found another way. Off to one side a side vent opened, choked with dangling roots, and beyond the roots showed a thin slot of daylight. Wren pushed the roots aside and peered through. The slot opened onto firm ground high on the slope, out in the clean air and the light. That was the way up and out, well above the reach of any tide. Wren wriggled toward it, glad of solid rock and open sky after the breathing dark of the bat cave.

choices.label: Squeeze out toward the daylight.

nodes.body: Wren climbed toward the light and air, up where a crack in the rock opened on the day. A few late bats flickered out past into the brightness ahead. The passage steepened and then gave way, and Wren came up out of the ground onto the open flank of the ridge. The whole island tilted away below, coast and reef and pale flats spread out under the racing cloud. After the dark caves, the sight of it was pure gladness. Better still, not far above, the grey canvas of the ridge camp showed against the higher rock. Wren brushed off the cave dust and set off up the slope.

choices.label: Make for the ridge camp.

nodes.body: The survey tower leaned on its headland, half its stone stair fallen away. Mapmakers had built it before Wren was even born, and Wren walked up to it with a kind of awe. This was where the craft had lived once, and the sea wind sang through its broken windows now. The door hung open on a ground room full of shadow. Outside, a spiral stair climbed the tower's flank toward the broken top, missing steps but climbable for a careful foot. A cellar hatch was set into the floor near the threshold, its iron ring furred with rust. And a keeper's quarters opened off to one side.

choices.label: Search the ground room.
Climb the outside stair.
Lift the cellar hatch.
Explore the keeper's side quarters.

nodes.body: The ground room was full of ruined instruments, and Wren moved through them like a visitor in a tomb. A rusted theodolite stood on its three legs, seized solid. Rotten ropes hung in loops from pegs, and cracked lenses lay scattered in the dust. In the middle stood a slate desk, and on it a logbook, swollen fat with damp. Wren eased it open. The last pages were stained but still just readable, a fading hand recording the final days of the tower. There was a story in there, half drowned. Read the swollen logbook, salvage a working instrument, or pry up a loose slab that shifted underfoot.

choices.label: Read the swollen logbook.
Salvage a working instrument.
Pry up a loose slab in the floor.

nodes.body: The logbook was Alder's, and Wren read the last entry twice to be sure. The true chart of the island, it said, had been carried up to the ridge camp for safekeeping before a great storm. Alder had meant to return for it. The words simply stopped after that. Wren copied them carefully, the whole sad, hopeful record, but the reading had eaten the light. When Wren finally looked up, dusk was leaning close. Home went a story of the lost mapmaker, faithfully copied, in place of a finished map. Some days a mapmaker carries back a life instead of a coastline.

Wren searched the ruined instruments for anything that still worked, and found one. An alidade, a battered brass sighting rule for fixing distant points. Its edge was still true, its little sights still swinging free. Wren turned it in both hands, hardly believing the luck. With this, from any high place, the island's whole shape could be fixed fast and sure, bearing by bearing. But a tool is only worth the height you carry it to. Wren could take the alidade straight inland toward the ridge camp, or carry it up the tower's own broken stair first, to try the view from the top.

choices.label: Take the alidade toward the ridge camp.
Take it up the tower stair first.

nodes.body: With the tower's find gathered up and stowed safe, Wren set off inland along the headland path. It ran firm and clear above the rocks, climbing steadily to meet the island's main ridge. The sea fell away behind, and the wind grew cleaner with every step. Far ahead, small against the grey cliff, the canvas of the ridge camp showed at last. It was the place all the day's clues kept pointing toward. Wren fixed the eyes on it and kept climbing, glad to be leaving the ruined tower for the living ridge, carrying whatever the old place had given up.

choices.label: Follow the headland path to the ridge.

nodes.body: The outside stair spiraled up the tower's flank, missing steps here and there, open on one side to a long drop. Wren went up slowly, keeping close to the wall, one hand always on the stone. Each step got a careful test before it took any weight. Two thirds of the way up, a whole span of stair had fallen, leaving a gap. Beyond it the next slab waited, close enough to reach with a bold stride if the nerve held. To one side, a crumbling ledge offered a slower way around. Wren stood at the gap, wind tugging, and judged the reach against the fall.

choices.label: Stride boldly across the gap.
Clamber the crumbling ledge.

nodes.body: Wren edged out onto the crumbling ledge, and it began to shed stones under both boots. Small pieces skittered off into the long drop below. That was the whole answer, right there. There was nothing to do but retreat, slowly, hands flat on the wall, heart pounding, back to the solid part of the stair. A ledge that crumbles when you test it is a ledge that says no, and a wise climber listens. Wren climbed carefully back down, legs shaking with leftover fear. The tower kept its high view to itself, and that corner of the island got drawn from guesswork below.

From the tower top the island showed its bones, and Wren turned slowly, drinking it in. The long spine of the ridge, the pale curve of the flats, the white line of the reef. And there, tucked in its ring of cliffs, the bright still water of a hidden cove. A surveyor could take rough bearings from up here and get the shape half right. But the true summit stood higher still, away across the ridge, reached only by way of the camp. A rough shape was not a finished chart. Cross the ridge toward the camp, climb down toward the cove, or examine the old beacon brazier.

choices.label: Cross the ridge toward the camp.
Climb down toward the hidden cove.
Examine the old beacon brazier.

nodes.body: The way from the tower to the main ridge ran along a knife of rock, the sea falling away on both sides. It was thrilling and slow. Wren walked it with care, eyes on the footing, keeping low where the wind gusted hardest, never hurrying. It was no place to be careless, and no place to be timid either, only steady. The rock underfoot was sound, and the view on either hand was enormous. Little by little the knife broadened and softened. At last it joined the wide grassy bench where the camp trail began, and Wren stepped off the narrow rock with a long breath of relief.

choices.label: Join the camp trail.

nodes.body: The cellar was dry and cool, and Wren climbed down into it to find the surveyors' old stores. Coils of rope hung from pegs, sound enough to trust. Tins of oil and food stood ranked on a shelf, and a lamp sat ready by the stair. This was a place stocked for long, careful work, everything a mapmaker might need. And rolled against the far wall, tied with a faded ribbon, stood a leather tube of the kind used to carry charts. Wren's heart quickened. It might hold the very map the whole island seemed to whisper about. Open the leather tube, or take rope and oil and climb back out.

choices.label: Open the leather tube.
Take rope and oil and climb back out.

nodes.body: The leather tube held a chart, and Wren unrolled it with careful hands, hardly breathing. But it was half finished and badly water-stained, Alder's abandoned draft. The coast was begun and the high ground left blank. A note in the corner explained it plainly. The good copy, it said, had been carried to the ridge camp, and this rough one left behind. Still, it showed enough. The safe path up toward the ridge was sketched clear, a gift from the mapmaker who came first. Follow the draft's guidance inland toward the camp, or sit down and copy the whole ruined thing in full.

choices.label: Follow the draft toward the ridge camp.
Sit and copy the ruined draft in full.

nodes.body: Wren sat in the cool cellar and copied Alder's ruined draft, reading each water-stained line and setting it down fresh. It was real work, careful and true. But when it was done, Wren looked at the page and felt the flaw in it. This was Alder's map, not Wren's. Every line was traced from someone else's seeing, and the blank half was still blank. The day ended with a faithful copy of another mapmaker's unfinished island and no new ground of Wren's own. A copy is a kind of honoring, Wren thought, climbing out at dusk, but it is not the same as looking with your own eyes.

Wren coiled a length of good rope over one shoulder and stowed a tin of oil, ready now for cliff or cave. A prepared mapmaker fears fewer places. And there, chalked faintly on the cellar wall by the door, was a surveyor's line, a straight guide mark pointing out and away. It ran along a marked path toward the high ground of the ridge, left to speed the next person on. Wren followed the chalk line up out of the cellar and into the light, rope swinging. Then Wren set off along the marked way inland, glad to be moving toward the ridge with the right gear.

choices.label: Follow the chalk line to the ridge.

nodes.body: The tidal flats stretched wide and mirror-bright, and Wren stepped out onto them warily. Near shore the sand was firm underfoot. Farther out it turned to soft grey ooze where the sea had drawn far back. Everyone at the Chart House knew the rule of such places. The water would return, and it would come in faster than a person could run. So a mapmaker on the flats keeps one eye always on the sea. A distant sandbar showed far out, pale with something wrecked. Firm channel edges threaded across the mud like roads. The strand line near shore glittered with treasures, and dark mussel beds crunched off to one side.

choices.label: Wade toward the distant sandbar.
Trace the firm channel edges.
Search the strand line near shore.
Pick across the dark mussel beds.

nodes.body: The sandbar was a treasure for a mapmaker, littered with old wreck timber. Half buried in it lay the whole ribbed hull of a boat gone to bones. It would draw beautifully. But Wren felt the change underfoot before seeing it, the sand darkening as water crept up from below. Out at the edge of sight, the sea had already turned and begun its long slide back in. That was the warning. There were minutes here, not hours, and the flats were no place to misjudge them. Sketch fast and run for shore while the running was still good, or linger over the wreck a little too long.

choices.label: Sketch fast, then run for shore.
Linger over the wreck too long.

nodes.body: The sketch was good, careful and true, and the run was simply not fast enough. Out here the sea fills the low channels first, cutting a person off before the flats are even wet. That was exactly what happened. Wren waded back chest-deep through a channel that had been dry sand minutes before. The sketchbook rode high overhead in both hands, and the feet felt for the bottom. Wren made it, soaked and shaking, and that was the thing that mattered. But the rest of the day went to drying out on the shore. The flats stayed a bright blank, a lesson written in cold seawater about the speed of a turning tide.

Wren sketched the wreck in furious strokes, eyes flicking between the pencil and the creeping water. The moment the drawing was true enough, Wren ran. Boots pounding the darkening sand, satchel bouncing, Wren reached firm ground just in time. The first thin sheet of water slid hissing across the flats behind, filling the way back. That was how it was done, fast and decided, respecting the sea's speed instead of testing it. Wren stood on the safe sand, chest heaving, grinning at the near thing of it. The flats were drawn and the feet were dry. Ahead, a green gap opened in the shoreline where the land climbed inland.

choices.label: Follow the green gap inland.

nodes.body: The green gap was the mouth of a valley, where a stream came tumbling down to meet the sea. Wren followed it up on the firm dry banks, glad of solid ground after the flats. The stream led inland and slightly up. As Wren climbed, the air grew cooler, and a sound came drifting down to meet the water. It was the steady rush of falling water somewhere ahead. Somewhere up this valley the stream was born, at a fall or a spring, the high heart of the island's water. Wren kept to the firm bank and climbed on, leaving the mirror-flats shining behind and below.

choices.label: Climb the stream toward the falls.

nodes.body: The firm channel edges made a natural road across the flats. Packed and sure underfoot where the soft mud sucked on either side, they were the way a sensible traveller crosses. Keep to the firm line, and the crossing stayed safe. The channels ran together as they neared the shore, gathering at the foot of a ring of high cliffs. There a gap of dark wet sand led inward, bared only by the low tide. Three ways offered themselves. Follow the channels on to that cliff gap. Cut inland up a firm rise toward the higher ground. Or detour out to the great wreck stranded on the bar.

choices.label: Follow the channels to the cliff gap.
Cut inland up a firm rise.
Detour to the great wreck on the bar.

nodes.body: The cliff gap was a slot of dark wet sand, and Wren understood at once what it meant. The sea only left it bare at low water, and in a few hours it would be deep under the tide. It was a door with a closing time. Wren stepped through quickly, boots sinking a little in the wet sand, into the shelter of the rock. And there, opening quiet and calm beyond the gap, lay a hidden pocket of beach. It was ringed all around by cliffs, a secret cove that no chart had ever shown. The water inside lay still as a held breath. Wren stood in the gap a moment, then went on into the cove.

choices.label: Explore the hidden cove.

nodes.body: The strand line was a museum of the sea, and Wren walked it slowly, reading the cast-up treasures. Shells lay heaped in drifts. A tangle of old rope, silvered by salt. A green glass net float, whole and gleaming, rolled up out of some fisherman's loss. And half buried in the wrack, catching the light, a corked bottle with a curl of pale paper inside. That stopped Wren's feet. A message, sealed and cast up here on this nameless shore. It could wait, or it could not. Open the bottle, leave it and read the shoreline, or climb the grassy dunes behind for a better view.

choices.label: Open the bottle.
Leave it and read the shoreline.
Climb the grassy dunes behind the beach.

nodes.body: The cork came free with a squeak, and Wren teased out the curl of paper with two careful fingers. It was a message, and the hand was one Wren was coming to know. A mapmaker named Alder had written it, plain and warm. This island is real, it said, and worth the naming. The full chart of it waits at the ridge camp for whoever finishes the work. It was an invitation across the years, sealed in glass and given to the sea in hope. Wren read it twice, the wind snapping the little paper. Set out inland now for the ridge camp, or keep the note as a fine find and stop here.

choices.label: Set out inland for the ridge camp.
Keep the note as a find and stop here.

nodes.body: Wren tucked Alder's message away safe and turned back toward the beach to wait for the ship. A mystery carried home in place of a map. It was a good story, the bottle, the invitation, the mapmaker calling across the years, and Wren would tell it well. But a blank chart is still a blank chart, however fine the tale that comes with it. The mate had asked for lines on paper, not legends. The apprenticeship would have to wait for another island and a steadier day. Wren sat on the warm sand as the light lengthened, the little note safe in a pocket, and watched the sea for the returning sail.

The hidden cove was a perfect pocket of calm inside the reef, and Wren saw at once why a mapmaker would love it. The water lay still and clear as green glass, sheltered from every wind. It was a place no chart had ever shown to the world. It felt like a secret kept for whoever was lucky enough to find it. A thread of fresh water trickled down one rock wall, sweet and cold. A path cut long ago by hand climbed away toward the ridge. And on a flat rock near the water, someone had drawn an old compass rose, its arrow pointing up. Map the cove and climb the rill toward the spring, follow the compass rose up toward the ridge, search the tide pools and sea cave, or rest a while in the calm.

choices.label: Map the cove, then climb the rill toward the spring.
Follow the compass rose up toward the ridge.
Search the cove's tide pools and sea cave.
Rest too long in the calm cove.

nodes.body: The cove was so calm that Wren sat down on a warm rock to sketch it. The stillness did its quiet work. The pencil slowed, the eyes wandered, and the morning slipped away almost unnoticed. One lovely corner took shape while the shadows crept round unseen. When at last a change in the light made Wren look up, the truth was plain and unwelcome. Only the cove was drawn, one perfect little pocket in a whole blank island. Wren scrambled up and gathered the satchel. Then Wren hurried off toward higher ground, knowing dusk was closer now than it had any right to be.

The hand-cut path climbed steeply away from the low ground, switchbacking up the rock past the reach of the sea. Wren followed it, glad of the old handholds and cut steps worn by feet long gone. Someone had made this way on purpose, and made it well. Halfway up, where the path leveled for a breath, a great slab of rock tilted toward the sky, its whole face pecked with small round dots. Wren stopped to look closer. The dots were not random, even at a glance, for they held a pattern, a scatter with real meaning in it. Study the dotted slab and read what it holds, or press straight on up toward the ridge.

choices.label: Study the dotted slab.
Press straight on up to the ridge.

nodes.body: Wren crouched over the tilted slab and read the dots, and understanding came like a cool wind. It was a star map. This was how the island's first people had found their way here, by night, steering under these very stars. The pecked dots marked the guiding stars themselves. Among them, a single deeper scratch marked the island's high peak beneath them, the mark to steer toward. It was navigation older than any chart, older than the Chart House. A whole way of finding the world, written in stone. It was a wonder worth a whole page. Copy it quickly and climb on, or copy it in full and go no further.

choices.label: Copy it quickly and climb on.
Copy it in full and go no further.

nodes.body: Wren filled a page with the star slab, every guiding dot and the deep-scratched peak beneath them. Around it went the shape of the island, drawn as the first people must have seen it from the sea. It was the oldest map of all, drawn in stars. But when the last dot was down, the light had turned, and the long climb to the ridge was too far now. The chart of the coast stayed unfinished. Yet what Wren carried home instead was no small thing. When the Chart House keepers gathered round the page and understood it, they fell quiet, then leaned close, the way people do before something truly old.

The cut path topped out at last on the broad grassy bench of the main ridge. The change was sudden and total. Wind and cloud and open sky, all at once, after the still green hush below. Wren stood in the buffeting air, hair whipping, and felt the whole island rise around and above. From here the way was plain. The stony camp trail lifted away up the ridge toward the grey canvas of the shelter. Every clue on the island had been pointing to it all day. Wren pulled the satchel strap tight against the wind, fixed the eyes on that distant grey shape, and started up.

choices.label: Climb the camp trail.

nodes.body: The spring was the island's heart, and Wren felt it the moment the ferns parted. A cold clear pool welled up quietly under the green shade. Water rose from the rock as if the island itself were breathing it out. Every stream on the whole island began here, at this one still source. Wren knelt and drank, and the cold of it went right through. Whoever had drawn the first ochre map had marked this very place. Standing over it now, Wren could finally see how the land fitted together, water running five ways to five shores. A path climbed on toward the ridge and its camp. Fill the flask and climb toward the camp, trace the streams to understand the whole island, or linger mapping every single one.

choices.label: Fill the flask and climb toward the ridge camp.
Trace the streams to understand the whole island.
Linger mapping every single stream.

nodes.body: Wren set out to trace every stream from the spring, and found the task had no bottom. Each rill split into two smaller ones, and each of those into more. Down every fold of the hillside they went, branching and branching, endless. The pencil chased them and never caught up. By the time Wren looked up, the sun had swung well past noon. The map was a beautiful tangle of blue lines with no coast to hold them together. It was lovely and useless, a middle with no edge. Wren rolled it up and headed down at last, humbled by the endlessness of running water.

Wren sat with the streams a while and worked out the island's whole drainage, the way a real surveyor does. Every valley, every shore, every trickle traced back to this one welling pool. The connections came clear one by one, until the whole living web of the water lay plain. Wren pencilled it into a clean, sure sketch, spare and true. It was the kind of underlying frame that would anchor a finished chart and make every later line sit right. Quiet, satisfying work, the bones of understanding a place. Then the climb to the camp called, up out of the ferns toward the high ground, and Wren rose to answer it.

choices.label: Climb to the ridge camp with the water mapped.

nodes.body: The path from the spring climbed up out of the ferns onto open ridge. The grass lay flat and combed under the steady wind, and the sky opened wide overhead. Wren leaned into the climb, glad of the height gained. Little by little the ground eased, and the path leveled through a high meadow bright with flowers. A few wild goats grazed there, sheltered somehow from the worst of the wind. They lifted their heads to watch Wren pass, then bent again to the grass. It was the gentlest ground of the whole day. Ahead, across the meadow, the camp trail lifted away toward the shelter on the ridge.

choices.label: Cross the high meadow toward the camp.

nodes.body: The high meadow was the gentlest ground all day, and Wren walked it gratefully. The boots went quiet in the soft grass, wild goats grazing unbothered nearby. It was ringed by an old tumbled cairn, a boundary marker some surveyor must have raised long ago. Its stones were fallen, but its purpose was still plain. That strange old cairn tugged at Wren's curiosity, the way a made thing always does in a wild place. But the camp trail lifted away on the far side, plain and calling, and the day was not long. Cross the meadow to the camp trail and climb on, or step aside to investigate the old boundary cairn.

choices.label: Cross the meadow to the camp trail.
Investigate the old boundary cairn.

nodes.body: Wren went to the tumbled cairn and read it. Among the fallen stones lay a flat capstone, and scratched into its weathered face was a survey mark and an arrow. It was one of Alder's boundary points, set here to fix the very middle of the island. Wren brushed the lichen away and traced the mark with a fingertip. It was a small triumph, a fixed and certain point in all the wide wild ground. It tied the whole island together from its center. Alder had stood right here, once, and left this. Note the boundary mark and climb on to the camp, or copy the boundary survey in full and rest here.

choices.label: Note the boundary mark and climb to the camp.
Copy the boundary survey in full and rest here.

nodes.body: Wren copied Alder's boundary mark and the careful way it tied the whole island together. It was proper surveyor's work, exact and true. And then the meadow was so soft, and the sun so warm on the grass, and the goats so peaceful, that resting a moment turned into dozing. Wren woke with a start to a lower sun and a cooler wind. The loss was plain. The middle of the island was fixed and the edges were bare, and the day had moved on while Wren slept. Wren rose stiff and cross and hurried down toward the shore with only half a chart, the boundary mark small comfort now.

Alder's ridge camp was a low stone shelter under grey canvas, and stepping in felt like stepping into a story. A cot stood along one wall, its blanket folded. A cold stove sat in the corner. And on a plank table, weighted flat with smooth stones against the wind, lay a real chart. Half the island was drawn on it in a fine, sure, patient hand, the rest left open and waiting. Wren stood over it, hardly breathing. A tin trunk sat closed beneath the cot. And beyond the low doorway, the path climbed on toward the summit and the last of the height. Study Alder's chart at the table, open the tin trunk under the cot, or take the chart and start for the summit.

choices.label: Study Alder's chart at the table.
Open the tin trunk under the cot.
Take Alder's chart and start for the summit.

nodes.body: Alder's chart was honest and unfinished, and Wren bent over it, learning it line by line. The coast was drawn true, the caves marked, the reef and cove fixed with care. But the high ground and the far shore were left blank. Across that blank Alder had written a note in the small sure hand Wren now knew so well. Finish this, it said, and it is yours, mapmaker to mapmaker. It was a passing of the work from one hand to the next, a trust laid down across the years. The tools to do it lay ready on the table. Take up the tools and ready to finish the chart, or simply copy Alder's half chart and call it done.

choices.label: Take up the tools and ready to finish it.
Simply copy Alder's half chart and call it done.

nodes.body: Wren copied Alder's half chart neatly, line for careful line, and carried it down to the beach at dusk. It was a fine map, no question. But the blank half was still blank, and the hand that had drawn the true half was still Alder's, not Wren's. When the mate spread it out and ran a thumb to the empty summit, the frown said everything. A copied map is a story, the mate said, not a survey. A mapmaker is paid to look with their own eyes, not to trace another's. So Wren went home with a fine tale and a borrowed chart, and no apprenticeship yet, and understood the difference now.

The tin trunk held Alder's whole island life, and Wren lifted the lid slowly, feeling like a guest at something private. Inside lay fine pens ranked in a row, a brass spyglass, a signal mirror bright as new. And a journal, its ribbon marking a place near the end. Wren opened it there and read a line, and the journal simply stopped mid-sentence. It was cut off the day a storm took the boat that never came back. The spyglass and mirror could reach the distant ship. The pens could finish any chart in the world. And the journal held the rest of the story. Gather the tools and ready to climb on, or read the journal to its last page first.

choices.label: Gather the tools and ready to climb on.
Read the journal to its last page.

nodes.body: Wren read the journal, and it told the whole of it. Alder mapping alone, season after season, loving the island more with every page. Naming the hidden cove. Fixing the spring, the reef, the far wild shore. And at the end, waiting on the ridge for a ship that came too late. The storm rising, the writing growing thin and hopeful, then stopping altogether. The last full line was a wish, plain and open. Whoever finds this, finish the chart, and give the island its name. Wren closed the cover gently, the wish settling like a weight and a gift at once. Carry Alder's wish on toward the summit, or close the journal, moved, and keep it safe.

choices.label: Carry Alder's wish on toward the summit.
Close the journal, moved, and simply keep it.

nodes.body: Wren closed Alder's journal with a lump in the throat and decided some things matter more than a test. The whole afternoon went to reading every page. The loneliness and the wonder and the love of it. The long patient life of a mapmaker who gave the island everything. All Alder had asked was that it be finished and named. When the light failed, Wren rowed out at dusk with a blank chart and a full heart. Alder's story went home too, so the lost mapmaker would not be forgotten. There would be other islands, other chances at the place. But there was only one Alder, and Wren would not leave the story to fade.

Tools in hand and Alder's half chart rolled safe under one arm, Wren stepped back out of the camp into the wind. Everything a mapmaker needed for the work was here now. The pens, the glass, the fixed and certain lines already drawn, and the will to add the rest. It felt like readiness, solid and calm. Only one place remained now, the one place high enough to see and draw the whole island at once. Coast and cave and ridge and reef together, in a single sweep. The summit. It stood above the camp, dark against the racing cloud, close now and waiting. Wren settled the satchel and set out for the top.

choices.label: Set out for the summit.

nodes.body: Above the camp the path narrowed to a thread of worn grass between two long drops. Wren slowed and gave it full attention, because ground like this forgives nothing. The summit cairn showed dark ahead against the racing cloud, close now but guarded by the fall on either hand. A line of cairns marked the safe way through, set by a careful hand to keep a climber honest. Off to the side, a bare scree slope offered a shortcut straight up, tempting and quick. Wren knew which the cairns would say, and knew too how a tired mind talks itself toward the fast way. Follow the cairns the safe way, or try the scree shortcut straight up.

choices.label: Follow the cairns the safe way.
Try the scree shortcut straight up.

nodes.body: Wren tried the scree shortcut, and the whole slope slid away the moment any weight went onto it. A slow hissing river of loose stones carried the feet out from under. There was no climbing that. Wren crabbed sideways off it, breathless, scrabbling for the firm edge, and made it back to safe ground shaken and gasping. The shortcut was proven a trap, exactly as the cairns had warned. It cost time, and worse, it cost nerve. With the light leaning on and both legs trembling, Wren judged the summit a bridge too far for one day. The high chart stayed unfinished. Some shortcuts are only the long way to a fall.

The cairns led on to a notch in the ridge, a narrow gap in the rock. The wind funnelled through it so hard that Wren could lean bodily into it and be held up by nothing but moving air. It was wild and exhilarating, and Wren went through low and braced, one hand on the rock. The wind took some of the effort and none of the balance. Beyond the notch the ground opened out to the final rise. The summit stood close now, close enough to count the stones of its cairn. One last field of tumbled boulders lay between. Wren caught a breath in the lee of the rock and crossed toward the rise.

choices.label: Cross the boulder field to the rise.

nodes.body: The boulder field was slow, careful work, hand and foot from block to block, the cloud tearing past in ragged streams. Wren took it patiently, testing each great stone before trusting it with weight. There was no hurrying such ground, and no need to. Block by block, hold by tested hold, the summit drew nearer. At last Wren pulled up over the last tumbled boulder, and there was nothing higher left to climb. Only the bare crown of the island, and the sky. Wren crouched a moment on the last rock, catching a breath, the whole world falling away below. Then Wren gathered the will and the limbs for the final step up onto the top.

choices.label: Stand up onto the summit.

nodes.body: The summit was a bare cairn of stacked stones standing in a river of cloud, and Wren climbed up onto it and saw, at last, the whole island. Every shore, every reef, every secret cove and dark cave mouth and shining flat. All of it laid out below like a chart already half drawn in the mind, only waiting for the hand to catch up. It took Wren's breath clean away. This was the place to finish the work, the one vantage high enough to see it whole. But the wind up here was fierce, tugging at the paper, and the light already leaned toward afternoon. Every choice from here would count double. Set up to survey the whole island now, signal the far ship first with Alder's mirror, or just stand and take it all in.

choices.label: Set up to survey the whole island now.
Signal the far ship first with Alder's mirror.
Just stand and take it all in.

nodes.body: The view was so vast that Wren simply stared, turning slowly on the cairn, drinking in the whole island and the wide ring of sea beyond. Every shore, every reef, every secret, laid out under the racing sky. It was too much and not enough all at once, and Wren could not look away. Then a cold gust and a slant of gold light broke the spell, and the truth of the hour landed hard. There was no time now to draw it properly, only to scramble down before the dark. Wren reached the beach at dusk with a head full of island and a chart still mostly bare, having stood where everything could be seen and drawn none of it.

From the cairn Wren laid Alder's half chart on a flat stone and weighted it against the wind. The work came faster than seemed possible. Sight the ridge, and mark it. Sight the reef, the cove, the pale flats, and mark them each in turn. The island's true shape came up under the pencil line by line, the blank half filling at last. It was the finest hour of the whole day. But there was more to fix than the failing light allowed, and a mapmaker must choose what to nail down first. Take careful sightings of every coast, fix the reef and cove for safe landings, turn the glass on the wild far shore, or try to rush every detail at once.

choices.label: Take careful sightings of every coast.
Fix the reef and cove for safe landings first.
Turn the glass on the wild far shore.
Rush every detail at once.

nodes.body: Wren tried to draw everything at once, and the hurry undid it. Lines smudged together. One bearing bled into the next. Soon Wren could not tell which mark meant which, the page turning to a crowded muddle under the racing hand. There was no time to redraw it, no time to untangle the crossed lines, and the light was going. Wren carried down a chart that was full but wrong in places, honest effort spoiled by haste. The mate frowned over it a long time before setting it aside. A near miss, the mate said, not unkindly. Which is the hardest kind, because it means the seeing was there and only the patience failed.

ending.title: All at Once, and None of It

nodes.body: Wren worked steadily around the cairn, taking clean careful sightings of every distant point. The salvaged brass rule swung true from headland to reef to far pale cape. Each bearing was fixed, each one noted in its place. The island tightened into its true shape as the marks gathered, guesswork giving way to certainty. This was the craft at its best, patient and exact, the wind be hanged. When the last far point was fixed, Wren straightened with quiet satisfaction. The bones were all there now, every corner pinned down true. It was time to gather the sketches together and draw the finished chart, while the light still held.

choices.label: Gather the sketches to draw the final chart.

nodes.body: Wren fixed the reef and the cove first, the safe water where a ship could come and go. Line by careful line, the channels Alder had begun took shape at last. The deep water marked, the teeth of the reef laid bare, the calm cove opened to any captain who could read a chart. It was the most useful map on the whole island, the kind that keeps sailors alive. And with it done, there was still a sliver of light left in the west. Just enough, perhaps, to push the whole chart further toward the finished masterwork. Push on toward the full masterwork, or stop here with the safe-landing chart done.

choices.label: Push on toward the full masterwork.
Stop with the safe-landing chart done.

nodes.body: Wren rowed out with a chart that showed exactly how to enter the island safely. The reef, the cove, the tides, every hazard and every safe deep line drawn plain and true. It was not the whole island, and Wren knew it. But when the mate spread it out in the failing light and ran a thumb slowly along the clean-drawn channels, something shifted in the weathered face. This, the mate said, is the map that keeps sailors alive. There is no finer thing a first chart can be. And with a nod, the mate took Wren on. The whole masterwork could wait for next season. The safe way home could not, and Wren had drawn it.

ending.title: The Chart That Brings Ships Home

nodes.body: Sheltered from the wind in the lee of the cairn, Wren spread Alder's chart and the whole day's sketches out together. The stones held them flat, and the island came whole. Coast and cave, ridge and reef, spring and shore, every piece fitting against Alder's careful half like two halves of one puzzle. It was all there. Everything seen, everything fixed, everything understood. The work of the mapmaker who came first and the mapmaker who finished it, side by side, making a single true thing. Only the drawing itself remained now, the last and best hour of the day. Wren took up a pen and made ready to draw it.

choices.label: Draw the final chart.

nodes.body: Everything was ready, and Wren knew that how the chart was drawn now would decide everything. The seeing was done. The making remained. Wren could ink it bold and clean, dark sure lines that would read at a glance and last for years. Or pencil it soft and fine, laying in every small detail, every tide pool and orchid and cave. Or copy it twice, the way a careful surveyor keeps a spare, one to keep and one to give. Each way was honest, and each said something different about the mapmaker who chose it. Wren flexed cold fingers over the paper and settled on how to draw the finished island.

choices.label: Ink the final chart in bold clean lines.
Pencil it soft and add every small detail.
Copy it twice, one to keep and one to give.

nodes.body: Wren inked the chart with Alder's good pen, and it came alive under the nib. Every coastline sure and dark, every headland fixed. The compass rose crisp and clean at the corner. The cove and the reef named at last in a firm clear hand. It was bold and true and unmistakably Wren's own work, the day's whole journey set down permanent in black ink. No fog and no failing memory could ever wash it away now. Alder's half and Wren's half had become one seamless island, drawn to stay. Wren sat back and looked at it, and it was good, finished and strong. Only one thing remained. Sign the finished masterwork.

choices.label: Sign the finished masterwork.

nodes.body: Wren pencilled the chart soft and fine, and left nothing out. Every tide pool, every orchid on its rock face, the handprint cave and the star slab and the vanished village. All of it went in with a patient grey line, until the map was almost a portrait of the island. A thing you could fall into and wander. It took longer, far longer, and the light was nearly gone by the last small detail. But nothing was missing, nothing forgotten, the whole living island held on one crowded loving page. Wren blew the last dust from the paper. Sign the detailed masterwork now, or fuss over one last small detail as the sun sinks.

choices.label: Sign the detailed masterwork.
Fuss over one last detail as the sun sinks.

nodes.body: Wren could not stop adding to the soft pencil map. One more cove, drawn in. One more current, traced. One more tiny cave marked, one more reef, the hand moving and moving. The sun sank to a red rim on the sea, and the ship's horn sounded low across the water, once, twice. Wren ran for the beach at last with a wonderful, unfinished thing rolled tight, too detailed ever to call done. The mate took one look and sighed. A map you never finish, the mate said, is no map at all. Wren climbed aboard with the loveliest half-drawn island in the world, and the lesson that done beats perfect, every time.

Wren copied the chart twice by the failing light, working quick and sure now that the seeing was long done. One clean master would go to the Chart House, and one working copy Wren would keep. That was how the real surveyors did it, because a map is too precious to trust to a single sheet. One spilled lamp or one gust off a cliff could destroy it. So Alder's island was doubled, and made safe from any single stroke of bad luck, two true charts standing where an hour before there had been none. The pen moved steady in the last of the light. When both charts were finished, Wren laid them side by side and made ready to sign.

choices.label: Sign both charts.

nodes.body: The chart was done, finished and true, and there was one thing left to do. The name. Whoever finishes the chart, Alder's journal had said, should name the island. Now a blank banner waited across the top of the page for a single word. Wren held the pen over it and felt the weight of the choosing. A name would outlast Wren, outlast the mate, outlast the ship. Sailors would speak it who never knew the island had once been blank. Name it for Alder who came before, name it for both mapmakers together, ring the margins with the island's living things, or leave the banner blank and sign the survey.

choices.label: Name it for Alder who came before.
Name it for both mapmakers together.
Ring the margins with the island's living things.
Leave the banner blank and sign the survey.

nodes.body: Wren wrote Alder's Island across the top in careful letters and rowed out as the first stars came. The finished masterwork went into the mate's waiting hands. The mate read the name, then read it again, and understood the whole story folded into those two words. The mapmaker who began, the one who finished, the years between, the promise kept. For a long moment the mate said nothing. Then, quietly: the Chart House has its new apprentice. And Alder, the mate added, looking at the name, would be proud that the island has a map at last, and a name to go with it. Wren looked back at the dark island and felt the day settle into place.

Wren wrote both names along the banner, the one who began the work and the one who finished it. The masterwork changed hands as the light failed. The mate studied the perfect chart, the coast and reef and ridge all true, then the two names side by side at the top. A quiet spell passed. This, the mate said at last, is the finest first survey the House has seen in years, and the naming shows why. A mapmaker who shares the credit is a mapmaker worth training. Wren was an apprentice no longer, but a mapmaker, name earned and island named, the two hands honored together on one true page.

Wren left the banner blank and signed the survey in the corner instead, small and neat, the way Alder always had. The chart that went down to the beach was so complete and true that the mate could not find a single fault. The mate checked it corner to corner, twice, then set it down with something like wonder. The name can wait for the House to choose, the mate said, but the place cannot. This is the best survey a first islander has ever brought back to me. And there on the darkening beach, the mate offered Wren the apprenticeship on the spot, on the plain strength of the work itself.

With two finished charts in hand, master and working copy both, Wren faced one last small choice. The boat waited at the water's edge, the mate ready in the stern. How to present them. Wren could hand over both together, openly, as plain proof of a surveyor's care. Or give one and quietly keep the spare, the first sheet of a mapmaker's own private library. Both were honest. One showed the mate everything. One kept a little for the road ahead. Wren weighed the two rolled charts, one in each hand. Present both to show a surveyor's care, or give one and keep the spare quietly.

choices.label: Present both to show a surveyor's care.
Give one and keep the spare quietly.

nodes.body: Wren handed the mate both charts, master and working copy, exactly as a seasoned surveyor would. A map is too precious to trust to a single sheet, Wren explained, so a careful mapmaker always keeps a spare. The mate turned them over, one and then the other, and looked up with plain surprise. A first islander, thinking like a professional. That, the mate said, is judgement, and judgement is rarer than skill. So Wren won the apprenticeship for the thinking as much as the drawing. Alder's island went home saved twice over, two true charts against the losses of the world. It was, the mate allowed, a very good day's work.

Wren gave the mate one clean chart and kept the spare tucked quietly away. The map that changed hands was fine enough on its own to earn a place at the Chart House. The mate spread it, nodded slowly over the true coast and the fixed reef, and welcomed Wren aboard. But the kept copy stayed Wren's own, rolled small and safe. It was the first sheet of a mapmaker's private library, a thing to keep and learn from and one day pass on. Alder's island was doubled now, one half given to the world and one half quietly, proudly Wren's. It felt like a beginning, which was exactly what it was.

The ship was a far white speck standing off the reef, patient, waiting for dusk. Wren remembered Alder's signal mirror warm in the satchel. With it, the low sun could be thrown straight across the water at that distant sail. A flash, in the old distress rhythm, might call the boat in early to the sheltered cove. Grown hands with it, in time to help the stranded creatures Wren had spotted from the heights. The seal pup in the warm cave, the turtle out on the reef. It would cost the finishing of the chart, or most of it. Aim the mirror and flash the ship, or signal quickly and turn back to finish the chart.
